topic: Two friends both apply for the same job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an irritating, persistent drone that seemed to vibrate directly in Elias's skull. He stared at the application form, the crisp white paper mocking him with its perfect alignment. Another submission for the Senior Analyst position. He traced the border of the designated section with a fingertip, the skin feeling strangely cold. Beside him, Liam was humming a cheerful, off-key tune, his energy a stark, unwelcome contrast to Elias’s internal slump. "You going to tackle that behemoth, El?" Liam asked, leaning over with an easy grin. Elias just blinked slowly, the effort of forming a response feeling monumental. He felt a heavy cloak settling around his shoulders, a profound sense of weariness that made even the act of breathing feel like a conscious expenditure of energy. He crumpled the corner of the form slightly, the sound unnervingly loud in the quiet corner of the cubicle farm.

[story 2]

Sarah reread the job description for the marketing coordinator role for the fifth time. Her jaw was clenched so tightly her molars ached. Every sentence seemed deliberately obtuse, designed to weed out anyone lacking the requisite 'spark.' She picked up her pen, but her hand shook, the ink threatening to bleed across the page. Outside the window, the city seemed to move with a relentless, indifferent pace, mirroring the sluggish crawl in her own spirit. She glanced over at Mark, who was animatedly discussing weekend plans, his laughter bright and effortless. Sarah managed a tight, brittle smile that didn't reach her eyes. "Yeah," she managed, the word thin and reedy. She didn't want to apply; the thought of putting herself through the ritual felt like wading through thick, cold mud. She just wanted the pressure to stop, the expectation to lift, but the weight of the expectation was crushing her chest.

[story 3]

From the worn leather of his backpack, David pulled out the final draft. The sheer act of typing felt like an immense physical burden. He kept pausing, deleting sentences only to rewrite them, the screen reflecting back a pale, drawn version of himself. He watched his reflection in the dark monitor—hollow-eyed, shoulders slightly hunched. He felt profoundly disconnected from the ambition that usually fueled him; today, it was just a dull, persistent ache behind his ribs. He heard Chloe across the room tapping her foot impatiently. "Are you done yet?" she called out. David didn't look up. He just pressed the 'submit' button with a decisive, almost mechanical movement, the click sounding final and utterly devoid of satisfaction. He leaned back in his chair, the sudden stillness amplifying the hollowness inside him.
